quick_facts.label: Credential issuer
quick_facts.value: The Certified Strength & Conditioning Specialist (CSCS) is an NSCA professional certification for practitioners who apply scientific processes to evaluate, train and coach athletes for improved athletic performance.

quick_facts.label: Eligibility for India-based applicants
quick_facts.value: Applicants outside the United States and Canada may submit a photocopy of their final transcript, degree diploma, or certificate showing at least a bachelor’s degree as defined by the U.S. education system; current CPR/AED certification is also required for certification.

quick_facts.label: Assessment format
quick_facts.value: The computer-based examination has Scientific Foundations and Practical/Applied sections. Candidates must pass both sections and meet eligibility requirements within one year of the initial exam date.

quick_facts.label: Published exam fee context
quick_facts.value: NSCA lists CSCS registration at US$340 for members and US$475 for non-members; preparation materials are excluded. These USD prices are variable and should be checked at registration, with INR cost depending on exchange rate and payment charges.

quick_facts.label: Maintenance
quick_facts.value: The credential is maintained on a three-year recertification cycle through continuing education plus current CPR/AED, or by retaking and passing the examination plus maintaining CPR/AED.

sections.heading: Eligibility for physiotherapy and sports-performance professionals
paragraphs: For this international professional certification, an applicant needs a bachelor’s degree or higher, or must be a college senior at an accredited institution, together with current CPR/AED certification. A candidate may register and sit the examination before obtaining CPR/AED proof, but the NSCA will not award certification until all requirements are fulfilled.

For applicants based in India and other countries outside the United States and Canada, the NSCA accepts a photocopy of the final transcript, degree diploma, or certificate demonstrating a degree equivalent to at least a U.S. bachelor’s degree. Eligibility documents must be supplied within one year of the examination date; otherwise, results become invalid.

For an Indian sports physiotherapist or government physiotherapist, CSCS is best understood as an additional athlete-performance credential: its published scope covers athlete testing, strength-and-conditioning programming, exercise technique, nutrition/recovery guidance, injury prevention, and collaboration or referral to allied health professionals. It does not itself confer a physiotherapy degree or replace the professional requirements applicable to physiotherapy practice.
bullets: Bachelor’s degree or higher, or qualifying final-year college status.
Current CPR/AED certification is required before the credential can be issued.
India-based applicants should retain final degree evidence and CPR/AED documentation for NSCA submission.

sections.heading: Duration and examination timeline
paragraphs: CSCS is an examination-based certification rather than a fixed-duration academic course. The Scientific Foundations section is scheduled for 1.5 hours and the Practical/Applied section for 2.5 hours; the CSCS also has a 15-minute scheduled break between the two sections. Candidates must pass both sections and complete all eligibility requirements within one year from the initial examination date.

After registration, NSCA requires the candidate to sit the exam within a 120-day authorization window. Testing is delivered through Pearson VUE test centres; prospective candidates should check centre availability before registering, because specific appointments are shown after registration.
bullets: Both sections and all documentary requirements: within one year of the first exam date.

sections.heading: What the CSCS examination covers
paragraphs: The Scientific Foundations section assesses exercise sciences, sport psychology and nutrition. The Practical/Applied section assesses programme design, exercise techniques, programme implementation, and organisation and administration. The exam blueprint is derived from the knowledge, skills and abilities identified for safe and effective strength-and-conditioning practice.

The practical content is relevant to rehabilitation-to-performance collaboration because it includes sport-specific needs analysis, training variables and periodisation, movement preparation, resistance/Olympic lifting/plyometric/speed/agility techniques, performance testing and monitoring, recovery, facility safety, recognising unsafe training practices, and knowing when to refer or collaborate with allied health professionals.

Questions are multiple-choice, may use images, video, or case descriptions, and use recall, application and analysis levels. Candidates should use the current NSCA Detailed Content Outline rather than relying on the title of a textbook or a third-party coaching course.
bullets: Scientific Foundations: exercise science, sport psychology and nutrition.
Practical/Applied: programme design, exercise technique, implementation, testing/evaluation, and administration.
NSCA lists its preparation resources as optional; purchasing them neither is required nor guarantees a pass.

sections.heading: Registration and certification process
paragraphs: A practical sequence is to confirm degree and CPR/AED eligibility, study against the current Detailed Content Outline, create an NSCA account whose name exactly matches government-issued ID, register through NSCA, then schedule through Pearson VUE after receiving the authorisation-to-test email. NSCA states that membership is not required, although members receive a registration discount.

The passing standard for an NSCA examination is a scaled score of 70 or higher. If only one CSCS section is passed, the candidate has one year from that examination date to retake the failed section; a retake requires the applicable fee. Official certification follows only after both the exam and documentary conditions have been satisfied.
bullets: Check a nearby Pearson VUE centre before paying, but expect test dates/times to become visible only after registration.
Bring a valid, unexpired government photo ID matching the NSCA account name.
Submit degree and CPR/AED eligibility evidence after registration and within the permitted one-year period.

sections.heading: Fees and ongoing cost considerations
paragraphs: As published by the NSCA in July 2026, the initial CSCS exam fee is US$340 for an NSCA member and US$475 for a non-member. A one-section retake is listed at US$250 for members and US$385 for non-members; a retake of both sections is listed at US$340 and US$475 respectively. These are issuer-published USD fees, exclude exam-preparation materials, and may change; an India-based candidate should separately budget for currency conversion, card/bank charges, CPR/AED training, and any optional study resources.

Recertification also has a variable cost. For the 2024–2026 reporting cycle, NSCA lists a per-credential member/non-member recertification fee of US$65/US$90 for certifications earned before 2024, with lower prorated amounts for credentials earned later in the cycle. The required CEUs likewise depend on the original certification date.
bullets: Initial exam: US$340 member; US$475 non-member, as published in July 2026.
Preparation products are not included and are optional.
Review the NSCA fee page immediately before payment, rather than converting a historical USD figure into INR.

sections.heading: Career use and progression
paragraphs: NSCA identifies CSCS-aligned roles including strength and conditioning coach/coordinator, performance or sports-performance coach, high-performance director, physical-education instructor, and athletic administration roles. It also identifies rehabilitation specialists, including physical and occupational therapists, among professionals who may benefit from the credential. For physiotherapists, this supports a progression toward athlete testing, return-to-performance programming and interdisciplinary sports-performance work while remaining within the practitioner’s applicable professional scope.

After achieving CSCS, holders can maintain the credential through the three-year recertification process. NSCA also states that, after two years of full-time strength-and-conditioning experience, a CSCS holder may pursue its Registered Strength and Conditioning Coach (RSCC) designations; candidates should verify the current experience criteria before planning this progression.
bullets: Use CSCS as a performance-training complement to physiotherapy education and professional registration.
Maintain current CPR/AED and complete the applicable recertification pathway every cycle.
Consider the NSCA RSCC pathway after gaining the required full-time experience.
